other_observers: Found bird while alone. Doug Faulkner was first on seen thereafter... after one hour of nervous waiting on my part :o). Eventually 20+ people present; Yes
specific_location: Barr Lake State Park, northern side of lake.
description: Large tern, roughly size of Caspian Tern, with similarly colored mantle. Head white except relatively narrow tonsure of black feathers from just behind eye (but not meeting eye) back around nape. These feathers formed a short crest when ruffled by wind or raised (rarely) by bird. Eye appeared black Bill orange tinted yellow, thickness and length nearly what one would expect from a Caspian Tern, but a bit narrower. No black or dark markings on bill. Underparts uniformly white. Upperwings gray, similar in color to Caspian Tern, excepting primaries. The outer 2/3 of primaries or so where dusted with a darker gray. The underwing was mostly white, with some dark edging on the primaries. The tail was white, a bit more forked than that of a Caspian, but not as deeply forked as a Forster's. SizeBehaviors: Mostly stood amongst flock of Franklin's, Ring-billed, and California Gulls with a couple Forster's Terns salted into mix. Occasionally would preen. Every once in a while would circle around lake, plunge diving, obtaining prey (small silver fish about size of bill, presumably chad?) about 1/3 of dives.Call: not heardPlumage: adult basic
photographer: Photographer: Steven Mlodinow, Steven Mlodinow, Steven Mlodinow, Steven Mlodinow
